data.title: Is POET Technologies (POET) Cheap On Its 2026 Production Ramp Plans?
data.description:
Advertisement

AGM update puts POET Technologies production ramp and spending plans in focus

POET Technologies (POET) used its June 26 virtual Annual General Meeting to reaffirm that its optical engine production ramp is on schedule, with volume shipments targeted for the second half of 2026.

For investors tracking the stock, the company also outlined plans to expand capacity to as many as 1 million units per month by the end of 2027, supported by recently raised equity capital and planned equipment spending.

See our latest analysis for POET Technologies.

Against this AGM backdrop, POET Technologies’ share price has been volatile, with a 1 day share price gain of 4.37% but a 30 day share price decline of 25.99%. At the same time, the 1 year total shareholder return of 78.16% and 3 year total shareholder return of 129.46% point to stronger momentum over a longer horizon.

If POET’s production ramp has you watching AI hardware more closely, this could be a useful moment to widen your search with 51 AI infrastructure stocks.

With POET Technologies still early in commercialization, modest current revenue, recent share price swings and a large discount to analyst targets, investors now face a key question: is there genuine upside left or is future growth already priced in?

Preferred price to book multiple of 4x for POET Technologies: is it justified?

On a simple yardstick, POET Technologies trades on a price to book ratio of 4x, which sits below both its US Semiconductor peers at 6.4x and the broader peer group at 6.6x, despite all the volatility baked into the current $10.28 share price.

The P/B multiple compares the company’s market value to the accounting value of its net assets. This is often a common reference point for early stage or loss making semiconductor businesses where earnings based ratios are less meaningful. For POET Technologies, this means investors are currently paying 4 times its book value while the company is still reporting modest revenue of $1.4m and a net loss of $81.6m.

Given POET is currently unprofitable, has a negative return on equity and is forecast to remain loss making over the next 3 years, a lower P/B than the industry suggests the market is already factoring in execution and funding risks around the production ramp and future profitability. At the same time, forecasts for very high revenue growth and the AGM plan to scale optical engine volumes may be why the ratio is not materially lower, as some investors appear willing to assign a premium to the underlying technology platform despite current losses.

Compared with the US Semiconductor industry average P/B of 6.4x and a broader peer average of 6.6x, POET Technologies’ 4x multiple stands at a clear discount and underlines that the stock is not being priced as aggressively as many profitable chip companies with stronger earnings records.

data.title: Arm Stock Leads These Robotics And Automation Picks For AI Infrastructure
data.description:
Robotics and automation are moving from the lab floor into real factories, warehouses, and infrastructure projects at a time when many economies are juggling labor shortages, mixed manufacturing data, and stubborn input costs. With central banks watching inflation, supply chains adjusting to geopolitical tensions, and companies looking for reliable ways to raise productivity, investors are asking which stocks are actually turning this physical AI shift into disciplined growth. This Robotics and Automation Stocks screener focuses on companies that are pursuing automation while keeping balance sheets in check, and this article highlights 3 of the strongest stocks in that universe.

Advertisement

Ouster (OUST)

Overview: Ouster is a lidar specialist that sells digital sensor kits and software platforms used in autonomous vehicles, industrial automation, robotics, and smart infrastructure, including traffic systems and AI-enabled heavy machinery. Its product suite, from OS sensors and Rev8 lidar to BlueCity traffic management and Gemini perception software, is built to give machines and infrastructure precise 3D vision and real time data.

Operations: Ouster generates about US$185.33 million in revenue from the sale of lidar sensor kits.

Market Cap: US$3.44b

Ouster gives you direct exposure to lidar at scale, where the investment story is less about a single product and more about how its Rev8 sensors, BlueCity traffic systems, and software-attached bookings tie into real contracts in transport, robotics, and infrastructure. The company is still reporting losses, posting a net loss of US$17.47 million on Q1 2026 revenue of US$48.58 million, and it relies on higher risk funding and a high P/S multiple, so execution risk is real. If you want the full picture on how its growth case stacks up against the competitive, funding, and valuation risks, you will need to go deeper into the data and narrative.

Ouster’s lidar story is shifting from promise to real-world contracts, yet the stock still leans on higher risk funding and a rich P/S ratio, so the 2 key rewards and 3 important warning signs (1 is major!) could change how you see that trade off

Allegro MicroSystems (ALGM)

Overview: Allegro MicroSystems designs and sells sensor and power integrated circuits that help control motion, manage power, and monitor current in systems like electric vehicles, industrial robots, data center cooling, and energy infrastructure. Its chips sit at the heart of applications such as ADAS, EV inverters, and factory automation, giving the company leverage to broad trends in electrification and automation.

Operations: Allegro MicroSystems generates about US$890.10 million in revenue from the design, development, production, and distribution of various integrated circuits.

Market Cap: US$12.36b

Allegro MicroSystems has caught attention because it sits at the intersection of electrification, industrial automation, and AI data centers, yet remains in loss making territory with a reported net loss of about US$14.9 million on US$890.1 million of full year sales. Expectations of stronger earnings and improved return on equity contrast with a rich P/S multiple, heavy reliance on automotive customers, and higher risk external borrowing that fully funds the balance sheet. Recent analyst upgrades, index inclusions, and design wins in EV and data center cooling indicate growing interest, but the combination of margin potential, competition in China, and governance changes means you need to weigh both the upside and the fragility of the current setup before deciding how it fits your automation watchlist.

Allegro MicroSystems sits where electrification, automation, and AI data centers meet, yet its rich P/S multiple and loss making status leave big questions around risk and reward. The 1 key reward and 2 important warning signs might surface the one factor investors are underestimating.

Arm Holdings (ARM)

Overview: Arm Holdings licenses chip designs and related software rather than manufacturing chips itself, supplying CPU, GPU, and AI accelerator blueprints that power smartphones, data centers, cars, and robotics. Its architectures sit behind many of the processors used by major semiconductor companies, cloud providers, and device makers worldwide.

Operations: Arm Holdings generates about US$4.92b in revenue from semiconductor equipment and services, with meaningful exposure to the United States, PRC, Japan, Taiwan, and Korea.

Market Cap: US$366.97b

Arm Holdings may merit a close look if you want exposure to AI infrastructure and custom chips without picking a single hardware vendor. Royalty-rich designs tied to AI data centers and connected devices support earnings quality, and recent news points to demand for its AGI CPUs and partnerships with hyperscalers and enterprise software providers. At the same time, a very full valuation, reliance on flagship smartphones, heavy R&D spend, funding entirely from higher risk borrowing, and geopolitical exposure to China introduce downside risk if execution slips. Whether Arm’s role in AI compute sufficiently offsets those risks at today’s pricing is an open question and one where a deeper breakdown can be useful.

Arm’s royalty engine and AI exposure could be masking a very different risk reward profile than the headline story suggests, and the 2 key rewards and 1 important warning sign may reveal what is quietly shifting under the surface
